data.title: Meta Dips As New AI-Focused Super PAC Raises Stakes In Policy Fight
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

​Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) shares slipped 0.71% to close at $755.22 on Tuesday, extending weakness in the broader U.S. technology sector. The pullback coincided with the company’s announcement of a new political initiative—a federal super PAC aimed at influencing state-level elections and shaping the future of artificial intelligence regulation.

The American Technology Excellence Project, Meta’s newly launched super PAC, signals a dramatic escalation of its lobbying efforts. With more than 1,100 technology-related bills introduced at the state level this year, the company has committed tens of millions of dollars to back candidates who support policies favorable to innovation.

Meta price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Meta is presenting the initiative as bipartisan, underscoring its goal of defending AI development from what it views as regulatory overreach. By adopting a proactive stance, Meta is shifting from a reactive policy posture to an active role in shaping legislation—a move that could safeguard its innovation pipeline but also invite greater scrutiny.

Market implications and risks

For investors, the new strategy carries both potential rewards and risks. Securing a supportive policy environment could strengthen Meta’s long-term AI growth prospects, particularly as the company continues to prioritize generative AI and infrastructure investments. However, the decision to intensify political involvement could expose the company to criticism over corporate influence in elections, amplifying headline risk and regulatory pushback.

Shares have already reflected cautious sentiment. Technically, Meta is trading within a rising channel but is testing support levels at the 50-period EMA near $758 and the lower boundary of the channel. A sustained break below $744, aligning with the 100-period EMA, could extend losses toward $725–$713. Conversely, a rebound above $765 would signal resilience and leave room for a retest of the $800 region. The RSI at 44 suggests momentum is cooling, with scope for further consolidation before a directional move emerges.

Outlook and positioning

In the medium term, Meta’s fundamentals remain strong, with robust earnings and growth in its AI-driven ecosystem underpinning investor confidence. The company’s ability to navigate the political landscape, however, will play a growing role in shaping sentiment. Balancing aggressive lobbying with strategic communication will be critical in maintaining support from both markets and regulators.

Previously, we discussed Meta’s uptrend as one of the key beneficiaries of AI enthusiasm, with earnings growth and product expansion keeping momentum intact. The launch of this PAC adds a new dimension—policy exposure—that could define the stock’s risk-reward profile into year-end.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Gold Rally Driven By Massive ETF Inflows
data.description:
By Łukasz Zembik

Short Pause, Strong Rebound

The pause in gold’s (XAUUSD:CUR) rally after last week’s Fed meeting proved exceptionally brief. Prices surged in recent days, breaking above $3,780 per ounce, with today’s trading consolidating between $3,760 and $3,780. Since the start of the year, gold has gained an impressive 43%. While official commentary signals expectations for further Fed rate cuts, Fed Funds Futures remain stable, still pricing in less than 50 basis points of easing by year-end, in line with FOMC projections.

ETF Inflows as a Key Driver

This suggests that gold’s rally is not driven solely by monetary policy. The crucial factor lies in massive inflows into gold-backed ETFs. On Friday, Bloomberg reported inflows of nearly 27 tons — the strongest daily increase since January 2022 — with 19 tons going into the largest U.S. gold ETF, SPDR Gold Shares. Earlier this week, another 8.7 tons were added, bringing September’s total inflows to 88 tons. Strong institutional and retail demand is fueling the rally alongside concerns about Fed independence and rising geopolitical risks.

Overbought Market, Risk of Correction

With gold reaching new all-time highs, the market has entered overbought territory. This increases the likelihood of some speculative investors taking profits, potentially triggering a short-term correction. The pace of the current rally appears unsustainable in the long run.

Silver Follows the Trend

Silver has also surged, rising above $44 per ounce to its highest level in 14 years. The gold-to-silver ratio temporarily fell below 85, its lowest in 2025. As long as gold remains in a strong uptrend, silver has room for further gains, with the historical resistance near $50 from April 2011 being a critical reference point.

Original Post

data.title: The Bullish Case For Platinum
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

After fifteen years of stagnation, platinum has woken up in a big way with an impressive 50% surge over the past four months. Even more exciting, this bull market may just be beginning.

Platinum is a dense, malleable, ductile, and highly unreactive precious metal with a silver-white sheen. It’s also one of the rarest elements in Earth’s crust—roughly 30 times rarer than gold.

About 90% of global production comes from a handful of regions, including Russia’s Ural Mountains, Colombia, Canada’s Sudbury Basin, and especially South Africa, which holds the largest reserves. Due to its scarcity, only a few hundred metric tonnes are produced annually.

This limited supply, combined with its critical industrial and investment applications, makes platinum both highly valuable and a key precious metal commodity.

Thanks to its unique physical and chemical properties, platinum is heavily used in industrial and automotive applications. It plays a vital role in catalytic converters, laboratory equipment, electrical contacts and electrodes, platinum resistance thermometers, dental instruments, and the glass industry. Platinum is also popular in jewelry, especially when gold prices are high, as they are now.

With gold trading at $3,766 an ounce and platinum at just $1,480, many consumers—particularly in China—are turning to platinum as a more affordable yet elegant alternative.

Unlike gold and silver, which have long histories as monetary metals used in coinage, currency backing, and investment, platinum was only discovered and understood relatively recently in the 18th century.

It has seen virtually no use as money, and only about 8% of its demand comes from investment. The remaining 92% is driven by other applications: automotive catalytic converters (37%), industrial uses (30%), and jewelry (24%), as illustrated in the pie chart below.

Owing to its substantial demand from industrial sectors, automotive applications, and jewelry, platinum exhibits high sensitivity to economic cycles, significantly more so than gold or silver.

This sensitivity is one reason I’ve been less enthusiastic about platinum, especially as I actively anticipate and prepare for a major economic crisis. Consequently, I’ve favored gold and silver more heavily.

That said, I remain optimistic about platinum’s future and consider it an excellent portfolio diversifier for larger portfolios with a strong foundation of gold bullion for stability, complemented by some silver.

I want to highlight the remarkably small size of the global platinum market compared to gold and silver, a factor that contributes to its greater volatility and choppiness, while also presenting significant upside potential when conditions align favorably.

The total value of above-ground gold stock stands at an impressive $26 trillion, silver at $413.36 billion, and platinum at a mere $4.51 billion, as shown in the chart below. This small scale is one of the reasons why central banks and major institutional investors largely overlook platinum, favoring gold as their preferred asset.

The platinum market's volatility is further amplified by its heavy reliance on mine production concentrated in a few countries.

South Africa leads with 3.86 million troy ounces mined in 2023, followed distantly by Russia with 739,000 troy ounces and Zimbabwe with 611,000 troy ounces, while the U.S. contributed a modest 93,000 troy ounces.

This dependence on nations susceptible to labor strikes and geopolitical instability introduces significant uncertainty and price volatility to the platinum market.

One key driver behind the recent surge in platinum prices (which I’ll explore in greater detail shortly) and a cornerstone of the bullish case for platinum is the significant decline in supply from both mining and recycling. This supply has dropped by approximately 1.3 million troy ounces, or roughly 16%, since its 2021 peak, influenced by multiple factors.

In South Africa, the leading producer, persistent electricity shortages, labor strikes, escalating operational costs, and insufficient investment in new capacity have caused major supply disruptions. Similarly, Russia, the second-largest producer, has faced challenges due to international sanctions stemming from the Ukraine conflict.

Another contributing factor is the global decline in average ore quality, as high-grade deposits are exhausted, requiring increased effort and higher costs to extract the same quantity of platinum, which gradually erodes supply over time.

Additionally, the low platinum prices in recent years (as I’ll show shortly) have rendered mining less profitable, further suppressing mine supply.

While the global supply of platinum has declined over the past five years, demand has remained robust, even amidst concerns about reduced need for platinum in automotive catalytic converters due to the rising popularity of electric vehicles.

A key growth area for platinum demand over the past decade has been the industrial sector, which constitutes approximately 30% of total demand, as highlighted earlier.

This increase is driven by global economic growth, surging demand in glass manufacturing, the medical sector, and platinum’s expanding role in hydrogen fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs), a component of the green energy transition.

The shrinking supply of platinum, coupled with sustained demand, has driven the platinum market into a deficit of nearly 1 million ounces over the past two years, with a similar shortfall anticipated for the current year.

This deficit, and the growing recognition that it is unlikely to improve soon, has been an important catalyst for platinum’s remarkable surge over the past four months, with prices rising approximately 48% to $1,480 per ounce. I will explore this trend in greater detail in the upcoming technical analysis section of this report.

Not surprisingly, the platinum deficits of recent years have led to a substantial decline in above-ground stocks, dropping from a peak of approximately 5 million ounces in 2022 to an estimated 2.16 million ounces this year—a striking 57% reduction in just a few years. This development exacerbates the already tight platinum market, which is inherently small, with above-ground stocks valued at just $4.51 billion, as noted earlier.

This limited supply means that even relatively modest investment capital could acquire a significant portion of the available stock, driving prices sharply higher. For large institutions and investors, this would be akin to an elephant leaping into a kiddie pool, causing water to splash dramatically in all directions.

Now let’s transition to the technical analysis section of this report. Platinum has surged nearly 50% since its rally began on June 20th. After that sharp move, it paused over the summer and formed a triangle pattern, which it broke out of today. This is a bullish signal.

The final confirmation I am watching for is a breakout above the $1,480 resistance level, shown in light blue, which was established at the highs on July 18th and 21st. Clearing that level is important because breakouts from horizontal resistance carry more significance than those from diagonal levels such as the top of the triangle.

Several catalysts have fueled platinum’s recent breakout after 15 years of stagnation. Chief among them is the powerful bull market in gold and now silver, both of which have reignited investor enthusiasm for the precious metals sector.

Additionally, as discussed earlier, the increasing recognition that platinum market deficits are likely to persist in the foreseeable future has played a pivotal role.

This supply tightness, coupled with the price surge, has driven lease rates in London and Zurich vaults to multi-year highs, signaling a scramble for platinum.

Furthermore, a significant portion of this recent rally is technically driven, a common occurrence when an asset breaches a key resistance level or price pattern, such as platinum’s three-year triangle formation that I will show shortly.

A look at platinum’s weekly chart shows that it broke out of a long-term triangle pattern in May. That pattern had been forming since 2022 and its breakout marked the beginning of a new bull market.

Triangle patterns indicate a volatility squeeze, where the asset is compressed like a coiled spring, poised to release with substantial energy upon breaking out.

Stepping back to examine the longer-term monthly chart reveals that platinum recently broke above the $1,200 to $1,300 resistance zone, which is a clear sign of strength. That zone has now turned into support.

The next major target and challenge lies in the $1,700 to $1,900 resistance zone overhead. A breakout above that level would provide another key bullish confirmation.

So far, I have outlined the bullish fundamental case for platinum, focusing on the persistent deficits, as well as the technical case, and now I aim to demonstrate how undervalued platinum remains across various metrics. The potential for its price to realign with historical norms further bolsters the bullish outlook, even without any changes in the supply and demand dynamics.

The first metric I will highlight is the real or inflation-adjusted price of platinum, tracing back to the 1960s. As depicted in the chart below, despite the recent surge, platinum remains near the lower end of its historical real price range.

With its current price at $1,480 per ounce, it is roughly half the approximately $3,000 it reached during the late 1970s and late 2000s. This indicates that platinum has considerable potential for further price appreciation.

Moreover, the historically low prices over the past decade have been a major factor behind the decline in global mine output. At those levels, mining became far less profitable, especially with the sharp increase in operating costs caused by inflation.

Additional evidence of platinum’s current undervaluation is evident in the platinum-to-U.S. M2 money supply ratio, which is near its lowest levels over the past five decades. I frequently compare asset prices to the M2 money supply to assess whether they are keeping pace with monetary inflation, and in this instance, platinum has clearly lagged.

(In this chart, I indexed the ratio to a base value of 100 in the year 1969 to create a more intuitive and visually meaningful price scale.)

The resurgence of M2 money supply growth in the U.S. and globally since late-2023 has been a significant catalyst for the broader precious metals bull market during this period.

Comparing precious metals such as platinum and silver to the leading precious metal, gold, provides valuable insight into their relative valuation, whether they are historically undervalued, overvalued, or fairly priced.

In this case, platinum is trading near its lowest levels relative to gold since the 1960s, reinforcing the view that it is undervalued and offers substantial potential for future price appreciation.

Since the start of the millennium, gold and silver have dramatically outperformed platinum, contributing to its current undervaluation relative to gold.

Over this period, gold has gained around 1,400%, silver approximately 750%, while platinum has risen by just over 300%. Nevertheless, this condition is likely to reverse as platinum’s bull market gains traction and its valuations return to historical norms.

Another significant catalyst poised to drive all precious metals, including platinum, higher is a potential bear market in the U.S. dollar.

The dollar and commodities, including precious metals, typically move inversely, meaning a bearish trend in the dollar is bullish for commodities, and vice versa.

In April, the U.S. Dollar Index broke below the key 100 support level, which has now turned into resistance. With the index trading below this critical threshold, the probability of continued weakness has increased, and the 90 level now stands out as the next likely downside target.

I foresee a substantial bear market for the dollar in the near future, driven by its extreme overvaluation relative to more than a century of historical data. Such pronounced overvaluation has only occurred twice before, in 1933 and 1985, and each time it was followed by substantial dollar declines.

The dollar’s unusual strength in recent years has been a major factor keeping commodity prices, including platinum, much lower than they would ordinarily be. However, an impending correction in the dollar’s value should trigger a powerful bullish surge across the commodities sector, including assets like copper, gold, silver, platinum, and mining stocks.

Multiple metrics indicate that commodity prices are currently exceptionally undervalued, aligning platinum’s low prices with this broader trend (as well as silver’s). One key metric is the commodities-to-Dow ratio, which highlights that commodities remain significantly undervalued compared to stocks.

However, I anticipate this unusual condition will soon normalize through a robust commodities boom or supercycle—reminiscent of the early 2000s—and a notable correction in equity markets.

This aligns with my broader view that we’re in the early stages of a major capital rotation out of overvalued stocks and into still-undervalued precious metals.

In addition to the commodities-to-Dow ratio shown above, I created a long-term logarithmic chart of the platinum-to-Dow ratio, which reveals that the metal is currently at near-record lows relative to stocks.

Notably, it has recently broken above the downtrend line that has been in effect since 2011, marking a bullish development. It’s worth highlighting that the last time the platinum-to-Dow ratio broke above a downtrend line, at the start of the millennium, it led to an impressive nearly 500% price increase over the subsequent seven years.

Having outlined the bullish case for platinum, I now turn to exploring various methods to gain exposure to it. Options range from the most straightforward—physical bullion bars and coins—to exchange-traded funds (ETFs), futures and other derivatives, and mining stocks.

My preference leans toward physical bullion, supplemented with selective exposure to mining stocks for those with a higher risk tolerance, given their volatility. I favor holding bullion in my own possession as the most resilient strategy, especially in light of the challenging and turbulent global economic conditions I foresee ahead.

When it comes to bullion, as an American, I particularly favor the U.S. Mint’s 1 oz American Platinum Eagle coins.

However, other renowned mints, such as the Royal Canadian Mint, the UK’s Royal Mint, and Australia’s Perth Mint, also produce their own high-quality platinum bullion coins. For those seeking to purchase larger quantities at reduced premiums, platinum bullion bars are a viable option.

It’s advantageous to acquire bullion, including platinum, at low premiums, which may require sleuthing around among various bullion dealers. A valuable resource for this purpose is FindBullionPrices.com, which facilitates price comparisons across multiple dealers.

Alternative avenues for investing in platinum include exchange-traded funds (ETFs), with only two available in the U.S.: the more popular abrdn Physical Platinum Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:PPLT) and the lesser-known GraniteShares Platinum Trust (NYSEARCA:PLTM).

While ETFs offer convenience, I approach them with caution in the current climate due to their increased complexity and counterparty risk, which undermines many of the inherent benefits of precious metals.

Another option is futures trading; however, I do not recommend this approach, as it caters more to short-term traders than long-term investors, demands a high level of expertise, and carries heightened risk given platinum’s status as one of the most volatile assets, making futures trading particularly perilous.

Another avenue for investment is platinum mining stocks, which I am interested in; however, the limited number available in the U.S. often necessitates purchases through the less transparent over-the-counter (OTC) market or Canadian exchanges, requiring a higher level of investor expertise.

To summarize, there are compelling reasons to adopt a bullish stance on platinum, including the persistent deficits of recent years and those projected for the foreseeable future, the favorable technicals such as the recent breakout, its demonstrated undervaluation relative to inflation, gold, the M2 money supply, and stocks, as well as the strong possibility of an impending U.S. dollar bear market, which could soon trigger another commodities supercycle akin to the early 2000s—a development that would be highly bullish for precious metals, including platinum.

Although platinum exhibits significant volatility and sensitivity to economic cycle fluctuations, I believe it deserves a place in portfolios, provided they are built on a solid foundation of gold bullion and supplemented with some silver, alongside high-quality mining stocks for enhanced upside potential.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. Q3 GDP Nowcasts Indicate Solid Growth After Fed Rate Cut
data.description:
US economic growth is still on track to expand at a moderate pace in the third quarter, based on the median estimate for a set of nowcasts compiled by CapitalSpectator.com. The upbeat outlook follows last week’s decision by the Federal Reserve to cut its target interest rate to address concerns that the labor market is cooling.

Economic growth is projected to increase at an annualized 2.1% pace for the July-through-September period, according to the median nowcast. The estimate marks a substantially softer rise compared with the strong 3.3% increase reported for Q2. The Bureau of Economic Analysis is scheduled to publish its initial Q3 GDP report on October 30. Today’s update of the median estimate is unchanged from the previous estimate, published Sep. 16.

The Atlanta Fed’s widely followed GDPNow estimate, one of the components of the median estimate, is the upside outlier, indicating a strong 3.3% increase for Q3 (as of Sep. 17)—matching the pace in Q2. Assuming the GDPNow data is correct, the Fed rate cut last week looks premature for an economy that’s still expanding robustly.

By contrast, yesterday’s release of PMI data, another input for the median estimate, suggests economic activity is cooling in September. Although the pace downshifted for a second month, “Further robust growth of output in September rounds off the best quarter so far this year for US businesses,” says Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Although there’s a fair amount of divergence among the individual nowcasts, the prevailing feature for most (per the median estimate): recession risk still looks low via the expected Q3 economic profile in GDP terms.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Virtus Duff & Phelps Global Real Estate Securities Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Policy uncertainty related to global trade remained high during the quarter, but moved in a positive direction. Long-term U.S. bond yields were relatively stable, as recessionary fears increased alongside growing global economic uncertainty, particularly around trade tensions and retaliatory tariffs from the Trump administration. The escalation of the conflict in the Middle East between Israel and Iran was also at play later in the period. For the moment, a tentative cease-fire appears to be holding.

The U.S. Dollar Index hit a 52-week low during the period and the DXY Index fell by about 7%. Increasing focus on the fiscal situation in the U.S. was a contributing factor. The recent passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act by the U.S. Congress also contributed to concern for the future fiscal situation of the U.S., as it does not meaningfully address the current or future fiscal deficits of the country.

Conversely, German Bunds rallied, with yields reversing some of the sharp increases from March. Further easing by the European Central Bank and Swiss National Bank also supported the rally in European bonds. Other major central banks, like the Bank of Canada and the Bank of Japan, remained on hold.

Against this backdrop, listed global real estate delivered positive performance for the quarter, but trailed the performance of global equities. Given the directionally positive news on global trade, the markets had a more “risk-on” posture, which helps explain the relative performance difference between the two. On a regional basis, Western Europe meaningfully outperformed, led by strong performance in continental countries on the back of the rally in European bonds. Asia Pacific also outperformed, led by Hong Kong and Australia, while the U.S. underperformed. Top-performing countries included Finland, Israel, and Germany. On a property sector basis, diversified, industrial/office mixed, and office outperformed, while industrial, health care, and self-storage underperformed.

Portfolio Review

The Virtus Duff & Phelps Global Real Estate Securities Fund outperformed the FTSE EPRA Nareit Developed Index for the quarter, with relative performance on country and property sector bases driven by security selection. Looking at attribution on a country basis, our overweight allocations to Spain and the U.K. added to performance, and our underweight exposures to Switzerland and Australia detracted. On a property sector basis, our overweight allocation to data centers and our underweight allocation to retail added to performance, while our underweight allocation to diversified and our overweight to residential detracted Stock selection in the U.S. and the residential sector contributed positively to performance, while stock selection in Belgium and the office sector detracted.

Analyzing security level attribution from a property sector perspective, our largest positive contributor to relative performance was our overweight exposure to Unite Group (OTCPK:UTGPF), a U.K. residential REIT. Unite Group performed well relative to its global residential peers during the quarter, as the company announced solid first quarter results, unveiled a new university partnership with Manchester Metropolitan, executed on asset sales at favorable values, and announced the potential acquisition of its U.K. student housing peer Empiric Student Property (OTCPK:EPCFF).

The next largest positive contributor to our relative performance was our overweight exposure to Merlin Properties (OTCPK:MRPRF), a Spanish diversified REIT. Merlin’s shares outperformed its broader diversified peers. The fundamentals in the company’s traditional commercial real estate assets remain solid, and the outlook for its emerging data center platform continues to improve.

Our largest detractor to relative performance was our overweight exposure to Ventas (VTR), a U.S. health care REIT. Ventas underperformed following somewhat disappointing first quarter results. The next largest detractor was our lack of exposure to LEG Immobilien (OTCPK:LEGIF), a German residential company. Residential real estate companies in Germany performed strongly during the quarter, as German Bunds rallied and the fundamental environment for residential property remained favorable. Our overweight exposure to Vonovia, a German residential company, also benefited from the same trends and contributed positively.

Looking ahead, we anticipate several key drivers for listed global real estate. First, additional central bank easing is expected, as real rates remain high. Second, earnings growth is projected to remain positive in 2025, building upon the attractive dividend yield that public real estate offers. Third, new supply is projected to continue to decline for most property sectors, potentially benefiting existing owners of high-quality real estate. Fourth, acquisition opportunities are expected to grow for public real estate companies. Lastly, the potential for further mergers and acquisitions is anticipated to provide a supportive bid.

As 2025 progresses, we have seen decreasing correlations for listed global real estate to the broader equity market. In our view, the decreasing correlations remain supportive of an ongoing and increasing allocation to listed real estate. In addition, the attractiveness of the daily liquidity that listed real estate offers continues to grow given what we see in both private real estate and private equity.

Please contact us to learn more about the strategy’s investment approach and current outlook.

data.title: Vertex Pharmaceuticals: I'm Downgrading To Hold On Drug Pricing, Pipeline Concerns
data.description:
Investment Overview - Why Vertex Stock Is Struggling For Growth

I have given the Boston-based Pharma giant Vertex Pharmaceuticals Incorporated (NASDAQ:VRTX) a couple of Buy recommendations in 2025 so far, once in March, when shares traded at $490 per share, and again in July, when they were trading at $479.

The stock is in fact down 23% since my first note, and 21% since my second, however, trading at $380 at the time of writing, its lowest value since December 2023. On a five year basis, stock is up 45%, which is almost identical to the gain made by the VanEck Pharmaceutical ETF (PPH), but less than half that of S&P 500 Index (SPX), which is up >105%.

Vertex has an extraordinary, almost monopolistic control of the global market for cystic fibrosis ("CF") drugs - according to its website:

Cystic fibrosis is a rare, life-shortening genetic disease. The treatment of CF has changed dramatically with the advent of cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator ("CFTR") modulator therapies that address the underlying cause of the disease.

There are around 109,000 people living with cystic fibrosis. Around 94,000 of those people have been diagnosed with CF across the U.S., Europe, Australia and Canada, which based on the genetics of the disease represents the significant majority of the global CF population.

Through formal reimbursement agreements and other forms of access, such as named patient sales or donations, Vertex medicines are currently treating more that 75,000 patients, representing more than 2/3 of people diagnosed with CF who are eligible for a CFTR modulator therapy.

In Q2 2025, Vertex reported revenues of $2.96bn - up ~12% year-on-year - of which $2.55bn was earned by its flagship CF drug TRIKAFTA/KAKTRIO (the drug is marketed as KAFTRIO ex-U.S.), and $157m by ALYFTREK, Vertex' latest product lunch in the indication, destined to replace TRIKAFTA as standard-of-care. Other product revenues amounted to $236m, which Vertex says includes $30m of Casgevy revenues, and $12m of Journavx revenues.

While the performance of Vertex' CF franchise was rock solid in Q2, and has been rock solid from its first product launch in 2012, allowing the company to grow revenues from $1.7bn, with an operating income of $11.2m, in 2016, to >$11bn in 2024, with an operating profit of $4.6bn, the pharma has faced questions around its ability to diversify its revenue streams with product launches in new indications.

Casgevy is Vertex' CRISPR editing gene therapy indicated for Sickle Cell Disease ("SCD") and transfusion dependent beta thalassemia ("TDT"), approved in December 2023, and Journavx its pain management therapy, approved to treat moderate-to-severe acute pain in January 2025.

Arguably, we can point to flaws with each drug that may prevent them from realizing peak revenue expectations in the multi-billions of dollars.

In the case of Casgevy, while the therapy may enable SCD and TDT patients to be pain free and free from hospitalization for years (as opposed to be regularly hospitalized with vaso-occlusive crises or because they are in need of a transfusion), treatment can take up to year (it is an ex-vivo cell therapy) as patients need to be conditioned to have bone marrow cells extracted, worked on in a lab, and reinfused.

The conditioning regime is similar in nature to chemotherapy and can cause infertility. The cost of treatment with Casgey is $2.2m, and if we take these things together, despite its being approved in the US, Europe, Canada, and many gulf states, its likely that the drug's commercial progress will be slow.

In the case of Journavx, efficacy is the potential issue. According to Vertex:

By inhibiting NaV1.8, JOURNAVX blocks sodium ions from entering pain-sensing neurons, disrupting the initiation and propagation of action potentials and reducing pain signal transmission from the PNS (peripheral nervous system) to the CNS (central nervous system)

The drug was approved based on two pivotal studies in patients who had undergone an abdominoplasty (tummy tuck) or a bunionectomy (bunion removal), easing pain as effectively as Vicodin, but failed to show superiority over Tylenol and a commonly prescribed opioid. As a non-opioid, it is not associated with addictive behavior, making it a favorable alternative, but only if it is truly effective - if the drug does not perform for patient's in a real-world setting, it may fail to fulfil analysts' expectations for $2.6bn of revenues by 2032.

In fact, when Vertex shared news that a Phase 2 study of Journavx treating acute pain after bunionectomy surgery failed to meet its primary endpoint of time-weighted Sum of the Pain Intensity Difference from 0 to 48 hours (SPID48), Wall Street dumped stock, sending the share price falling from ~$472, to ~$365 - a loss of 23%.

Vertex Buffeted By Trump Administrations Tariff, Drug Pricing Policies

With a current market cap of $97.3bn, and 2025 guidance for $11.85bn - $12.08bn, and GAAP combined SG&A / R&D expenses of $5.55bn - $5.7bn - We may conclude Vertex' valuation may be a little too high.

I calculate forward price to sales to be ~8x, which is higher than the sector average of ~4.5x (by my calculation), and price to forward earnings to be ~19x, which is actually somewhat lower than the sector average. Vertex is a phenomenally profitable company, although that could arguably become another issue for the company going forward.

Vertex was not among the pharma companies that were written to by the White House, and told to implement a most favored nation ("MFN") policy, i.e., making sure Americans are charged no more for their drugs than whichever country charges the least, but those that were include AbbVie (ABBV), Amgen (AMGN), AstraZeneca (AZN), Boehringer Ingelheim, Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Eli Lilly (LLY), EMD Serono, Genentech (OTCQX:RHHBY) Gilead (GILD), GSK (GSK), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Merck (MRK), Novartis (NVS), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Pfizer (PFE), Regeneron (REGN), and Sanofi (SNY).

Apparently, Alyftrek has a list price of $370k per annum, while Trikafta costs ~$346k per annum, yet in the UK, the list price is ~$22k. Does that mean Vertex will be put under serious pressure to slash its prices in the US, significantly impacting its profitability?

During a recent fireside chat at the Bank of America global healthcare conference, Vertex' Senior Vice President of Investor Relations Susie Lisa addressed a question regarding MFN as follows:

it is very hard to comment on because there are so few details. But we have very strong advocacy in D.C., a lot of planning going on in the event that this does come to pass. And I think also exploring potential options and offsets that we could implement.

And I will say that broadly speaking, because we have -- we're on our fifth medicine, it's not like we launched one drug 20 years ago and took annual price increases in the U.S., right? So we have said that after the statutory discount in the U.S. for Medicaid patients of 23.1%, I think it is, that we are broadly in the same line -- in the same range from an MFN-type basket of pricing.

There certainly would be challenges, but I think we're relatively well positioned versus some peers. And I think foundationally, as a company, we target very serious diseases where we're trying to deliver this transformative type of effect. And I think that often helps you in challenging reimbursement environment. So that's where we'll continue to focus.

The VP did also discuss potential growth in the CF market, however, pointing to:

the survival benefits that our CFTR modulators bring. A baby born today who starts on our medicines is expected to live into their early 80s, right? So that survival advantage is really driving overall market growth for us. And then we also continue to get approvals for our therapies into younger and younger patients.

Besides MFN, Vertex must deal with another headwind created by the Trump administration in the form of tariffs on imports into the U.S., and potentially, tariffs aimed exclusively at pharma companies, although VP Lisa played down any potential impact, commenting:

I think relatively well positioned versus peers. The majority of our manufacturing is done in the U.S. We're currently building a new facility with our partners, Lonza in New Hampshire for our cell therapy products, primarily type 1 diabetes. And to date, with what we know, there's been minimal impact

Nevertheless, these twin threats would concern me slightly if I were holding Vertex stock. A growing market for its CF drugs might offset losses from drug price cuts, but it would likely stymie the revenue growth that shareholders have become used to, while marketing and selling its drugs ex-U.S. may become complex and difficult, as cuts to prices in the U.S. may need to offset by hiking prices ex-U.S.

What Does Vertex Need To Do To Restore Investor's Faith?

There are a few things Vertex can do in my view. Firstly, with regard to approved drugs, I do believe that uptake of Casgevy will increase significantly and agree with Vertex management that this a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) drug in waiting.

I covered the reasons why I believe this in depth in a recent note on Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP), with whom Vertex has a development and commercialization agreement in place for the drug, with profits on sales split 60/40. Both companies are working on easing the conditioning regimes for patients, although there are no programs in the clinic yet, and a solution may be years away, but even despite this, the efficacy results from studies have been so impressive, it's my belief a trickle of new patients today has the potential to become a stream by the end of the decade.

With Journavx, I think a combination of Vertex' reputation, infrastructure, and ability to commit funds to marketing - the company reported a cash position of $12bn as of the end of Q2 - plus the fact the drug is an alternative to opioids, will see it too become a blockbuster drug, albeit its peak sales years may be affected by the intensifying competition in the NAV-inhibiting space.

Given headwinds discussed above, I'd not be completely confident that these two drugs are capable of driving Vertex' market cap valuation back >$100bn in the short-to-medium term. Luckily, Vertex has several other assets in its pipeline, shown below (slide taken from a Q2 earnings presentation) that potentially could.

Vertex paid $4.9bn to acquire Alpine Immune and its drug candidate povetacicept last year, and is confident that it can file for accelerated approval (approval based on an endpoint indicative of clinical benefit) in 1H26 for the kidney disease drug. Interim results from Vertex' RAINIER study of the drug candidate are expected early in 2026.

The slight issue here is that IgA Nephropathy ("IgAN"), the target indication, is becoming increasingly crowded. There are three approved drugs already -Tarpeyo (budesonide), Filspari (sparsentan), and Fabhalta (iptacopan), marketed by Calliditas Therapeutics, Travere Therapeutics (TVTX), and Novartis (NVS), and the Swiss Pharma giant expects to have a second therapy, zigakibart on the market by 2027, while Vera Therapeutics (VERA) hopes to commercialise its candidate atacicept, and Ionis Pharmaceuticals is also testing an RNA-based therapy. As such, Vertex is under some pressure to establish best-in-class credentials for its candidate.

Zimislecel is another therapy that could see an approval submission in 2026. On its Q2 earnings call with analysts, Vertex' CEO Reshma Kewalramani told analysts:

Recall, we expect about 60,000 severe type 1 diabetic patients may potentially benefit from this first zimislecel submission. In June, at the ADA meeting and concurrently in the New England Journal of Medicine, positive data for zimislecel in type 1 diabetes were presented that continue to demonstrate this cell therapy's transformative potential.

All 12 patients with at least 1 year of follow-up who received a full dose of zimislecel as a single infusion achieved ADA recommended target hemoglobin A1c levels less than 7%, freedom from severe hypoglycemic events during the evaluation period and greater than 70% time in range. Remarkably, 10 of the 12 patients at 12 months were insulin-free, a testament to the potential transformative benefit and durability of this therapy.

This is a hugely exciting drug, albeit it may be hamstrung like Casgevy by the fact it is a cell therapy, and may be subject to similarly harsh conditioning regimes. To make the drug cost-effective, will Vertex have to set a list price that puts off patients and health insurers (and the government)? Inaxaplin is another candidate that secure approval in kidney disease - APOL1-mediated kidney disease ("AMKD") to be precise, albeit peak revenue projections are modest.

In summary, then, to win Wall Street back over, Vertex cannot afford for its pipeline to fail, or present the kinds of flaws that may undermine the progress of Casgevy and Journavx, however, if we look "under the hood" and at the market opportunities in play, it seems hard to deny that similar flaws are present.

Analysis - Why I am Downgrading Vertex From Buy To Hold

Normally when I look into Vertex as an investment opportunity I typically begin by concluding the business looks over-valued relative to peers. For example, Vertex is more valuable than Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), despite BMY driving revenues of >$40bn per annum, and more valuable than GSK - $39bn of revenues per annum.

After looking at the peak revenue potential of the company's assets and its pipeline, however, I usually conclude that Vertex is indeed worth >$100bn market cap, as I can plot a path to >$25bn of revenues by the middle of next decade, with, for example, $15bn CF franchise, $2.5bn Journavx, $2.5bn Casgevy, $2bn povetacicept, and >$5bn zimislecel.

On this occasion, however, there are new headwinds to consider. Drug pricing primarily - how will Vertex justify having one of the highest net profit margins in Pharma when there is so much pressure to cut prices in the U.S.? Tariffs are another impediment, and then turning to the pipeline, while I have always been positive on that front, outside of CF Vertex' R&D credentials are not especially strong, and while its pipeline has great promise, it also contains significant risk. Is povetacicept best-in-class when there are several drugs with the same MoA approved and in development? Will zimislecel be undermined by its complexity (and potentially, the rise of GLP-1 drugs)? Is progress on a less harsh conditioning regime for Casgevy too slow?

In summary, I have always tended to look on the bright side when it comes to Vertex, but one of its greatest strengths - profitability - could become a vulnerability, in my view, in light of MFN, while the stuttering progress of Casgevy and Journavx, in particular, give me pause for thought around the company's ability to convert pipeline promise into commercial revenues of a scale that offsets dependency on the CF franchise.

As such, I am downgrading Vertex to a Hold, until some of the questions I have asked in this post have been answered. I don't foresee the remainder of 2025 being especially positive for the company, and will wait to see what the outlook for 2026 is like before considering a re-rating.

data.title: 5 Things To Consider As Government Shutdown Risks Loom
data.description:
On Tuesday, September 23, U.S. President Trump canceled a meeting with two top Democrats in Congress. This increases the risk of a federal government shutdown should Congress fail to agree on a funding deal.

In recent years, between 2019 and 2024, the government repeatedly used Continuing Resolutions (“CRs”) and omnibus bills to continue government funding. This contrasts with 2018-2019, when the government shut down for 35 days (between December 22, 2018, and January 25, 2019).

What are the macroeconomic considerations if the government shuts down? I continue to favor sectors that thrive on government spending. This typically includes companies in the defense and aerospace industries when providing coverage on the S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:IVV) (SP500) (NYSEARCA:SPY). A brief government shutdown would merely temporarily delay contractual payments to military contractors. Firms like Lockheed Martin (LMT), RTX (RTX), and Northrop Grumman (NOC) would not pause production or cut staff in that case.

In the Seeking Alpha quant scores above, NOC and RTX stocks have weak valuations. A price-to-earnings compression is a more likely pullback risk for those stocks.

What happens if a government shutdown is worse this time, in that it lasts for weeks or months? Why or how the political parties might want to draw out a longer shutdown is beyond the scope of this article. Instead, I will evaluate what a shutdown will mean for investors and the market.

#1) Economic Impact

Investors should manage a worst-case scenario of a shutdown lasting longer than expected. Use the five-week partial shutdown from December 22, 2018, that ended on January 25, 2019. The Congressional Budget Office estimated that the shutdown delayed $18 billion in federal spending. It lowered real GDP in the first quarter of 2019 by $8 billion. This accounted for 0.2% of GDP.

The CBO listed a lapse in discretionary spending among several departments and agencies. They were “the Department of Agriculture, the Department of Commerce, the Department of Homeland Security, Department of Housing and Urban Development, the Department of the Interior, the Department of Justice, the Department of State (and the related international assistance programs), the Department of Transportation, the Department of the Treasury, the Environmental Protection Agency, the National Aeronautics and Space Administration, and the National Science Foundation.”

Agencies required their employees to stop working. However, this excluded essential employees, who were involved in “activities for the protection of life or property.”

Back then, the federal civilian workforce totaled around 2.1 million workers. 800,000 staff worked in agencies that did not have appropriation. The government furloughed 300,000 (or 38%) of the workers.

The CBO estimated that employee compensation fell by $9 billion. From that amount, $3 billion represented compensation for furloughed staff, while $6 billion represented compensation for employees who needed to work (“excepted employees”).

The CBO said that the partial government shutdown had a negative macroeconomic effect. It lowered federal spending on goods and services temporarily. Even though it expected the GDP to rebound, the country still lost some unrecoverable GDP. It also temporarily affected the labor market.

Readers should expect that a shutdown would hurt employment figures in monthly labor reports.

#2) Treasury Bonds

A prolonged shutdown should not have an impact on Treasury Bonds, since the government can continue to pay interest on time. The recent breakout to $91, then a fade to $89, in the 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) has more to do with the Federal Reserve. On September 17, the FOMC lowered the target range for the federal funds rate by 25 bps, to 4.00%–4.25%.

In a more recent speech, FOMC Chair Jerome Powell lamented that the central bank does not have a risk-free policy path. The risk is two-sided, where the FOMC has a higher downside risk for employment. When interest rates neither lift nor hurt economic growth, it does little to help the job market. Powell said that the pace of job creation looks like it is running below a breakeven rate.

The Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ), Dow Jones (DJI), and S&P 500 traded lower on Tuesday in reaction to Powell’s comments on interest rate cuts. He said that easing too aggressively could lead to persistently high inflation.

#3) Delayed Economic Reports

Readers might not take delayed economic reports from the Bureau of Labor Statistics seriously. On Monday, the BLS said that it would delay its annual Consumer Expenditures report. It will publish the report on October 30 instead.

Historically, stock markets would react to inflation and non-farm payroll reports that did not meet expectations. A prolonged government shutdown would delay their report releases. That might lower stock volatility; the VIX ETF (VXX) is already in a downtrend.

#4) Benefits to the Cable and Media Sector

The cable and media sector should benefit from heightened attention from political parties. Still, shares of Disney (DIS) benefited from a week-long suspension of Jimmy Kimmel Live! In ending the suspension, Disney said, “We decided to suspend production on the show to avoid further inflaming a tense situation at an emotional moment for our country.”

That same night, Disney hiked streaming subscription rates. The ad-supported tier will increase by $2.00 to $13.00/month, while the ad-free plan rises to $19.00/month, up by $3.00. Investors may infer that a longer shutdown would invite more opinions from the government parties. That should increase viewership. However, this assumption requires customers not to cancel their subscription. Executives are betting that ongoing inflation is not hurting disposable income enough to entice customers to react to the price increase.

#5) Stocks Most Highly Affected

A shutdown might hurt defense contractor Boeing (BA) more than Lockheed or RTX (as mentioned above). Boeing lagged behind on its 777X certification schedule. Boeing shareholders would anticipate that a furlough for Federal Aviation Administration and Department of Transportation employees would delay certifications. That would hurt the company’s plane deliveries.

In Medicaid, a government disruption might hurt UnitedHealth (UNH). UNH is the largest provider of Medicare Advantage plans in the U.S. Furloughed Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services staff might delay claims processing, audits, and payments.

A shutdown is unlikely to disrupt government spending in the technology sector. For example, Palantir (PLTR) and Microsoft (MSFT) would eventually get paid after the government reopens. Still, the sentiment among market participants may worsen. They may re-evaluate the stretched valuations among the Magnificent Seven. In particular, Tesla (TSLA) trades at a P/E of 251 times.

Apple (AAPL) is relatively inexpensive at a 38.2 times P/E. But if sentiment weakens, investors might renew concerns about Apple’s lack of an in-house AI to justify a lower P/E.

Your Takeaway

Stock markets could previously dismiss the looming government shutdown. Each time, the government passed stopgap measures to continue funding.

This time might be different. Differences among government officials may be too great to settle. Investors should consider this worst-case scenario with risk management, especially when stock markets trade near all-time highs.

data.title: Valuations Matter: These 3 Companies Have Sky-High Multiples, So Watch Out
data.description:
The recently completed earnings season was good, with most companies reporting good, if not growing earnings. At the same time, while some churn is still going on about tariffs, most are not as punitive as originally announced. We also saw the Federal Reserve lower their lending rate by 25 basis points this week, with indications they may reduce it by another 50 basis points before the end of the year. These factors continue to support a highly valued (or possibly overbought per some recent articles here on SA) market. As part of this, a number of stocks have gone up by a large amount over the past 12 or so months and continue showing strength and buying. Is this positive momentum in these names, or do we have a "meme stonk 2.0" and "irrational exuberance" situation that should, at a minimum, lead to caution and more likely profit taking if not even bearish positioning?

Outsized Even 100% Gains This Year

A number of stocks that announced positive earnings and news recently are WAY up. These stocks included Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS), Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), Carvana Co. (CVNA), and others. Here is a modest table showing year-to-date gains, price-to-sales, and price-to-earnings for these stocks. (Table with P/Es and P/Ss).

Company Symbol YTD Gain P/E (TTM) P/S (TTM)
Hims and Hers Health HIMS 139% 73.3 6.33
Palantir PLTR 141% 604.6 122.9
Carvana CVNA 93% 97.59 3.15

All data from the respective Seeking Alpha Stock page, valuation tab.

These companies are showing earnings growth (except for Tesla), while all are trying to change or dominate industries. But are they "ahead of themselves" and FOMO/Meme stock momentum has them at nosebleed levels? Let's take a look at each one, state the positives, but also point out the risks and euphoria that may be driving the 2025 run-ups in each of them.

Hims and Hers Health

Hims & Hers Health, Inc. ((HIMS)) is the stock that actually got this article started. Twice this year, the stock has moved up rapidly around earnings, first in February and again in May, and just before earnings in August. After the February move up from $33.96 at the end of January to a high of $68.74, before retracing this entire gain and falling slightly lower. The company's stock then traded sideways and slowly dropped below $30 a share by late April.

Two significant events have happened since this low. The first is an announcement of partnerships and acquisitions that led to a rapid spike in share price. The second was the earnings announcements, which are positive news. In the span of a month, the share price regained nearly all the February gains, surpassing $68 a share twice between May 13th and 16th. However, since then, the share price has traded in bumpy rises and falls and closed Friday, 9/19, at $57.82. While not a new high, this is on the higher end of this year's share pricing. As seen in the table above, this is a rich valuation with a P/E and P/S over twice their peers, with P/E approaching three times industry averages. With the outsized upward momentum multiple times this year but with a steady decline after each of these run-ups, shares may currently be pricing in near perfection and also may slowly decline through the gap now and their next earnings. While Hims and Hers is definitely enjoying tailwinds, exuberance has likely driven the share price up and it's gotten ahead of itself.

While both the partnership announcements and the earnings are continued positives for the stock, the pattern in HIMS makes me cautious that further declines are likely. For full disclosure, I entered a series of bear call spreads in HIMS, all of which seem to have been too early. Time decay is also favorable for the out-of-the-money spreads layered into the strategy, but I have been on the wrong side of this trade for months. While I doubt the term is being used much anymore, I believe HIMS is the most likely of the stocks covered to be displaying "stonk" like behavior.

Palantir: Eye-Popping 400% Gain From 52-Week Low

For those who bought Palantir last spring when it was trading below $21, they have seen amazing gains. Like HIMS, Palantir Technologies spiked around earnings in February, retraced much of the gain, and then reclaimed those highs by May. Recently, the stock has spiked by another 30%! But which company is it—the one that was valued at $21 a share only 12 months ago or the one now trading at almost 9 times that? Palantir may be one of the poster children for the AI craze that has been in the market for a while now. Palantir has very good technology (I was exposed to it when the U.S. Department of Defense was an "early adopter"—actually seeding much of the development with GWOT money), and many of the company's contracts are "sticky". At the same time, a price-to-earnings multiple of over 600 means every dollar of earnings is being bought dearly.

While Palantir does have what I consider "sticky" contracts, they also have both large and small competitors. While I am not an expert to compare the applications, and others already do a far better job than I would, I simply want to do a valuation analysis of one alternative: Amazon. Consider buying Amazon Web Services for 100% of the share price of the entire company, basically getting the retail, streaming, and search businesses for "free". Amazon trades at valuations far below Palantir's. TTM P/E is 17X greater for PLTR, and TTM prices to sales are nearly 40x! While Palantir is growing from a much smaller base than Amazon, any multiple contraction from such lofty numbers could be very punitive to purchasers at today's lofty valuations. Conversely, Amazon has many avenues to execute well on, so that if one only performs adequately, there are other aspects compared to Palantir's one main business line.

Will Palantir continue to succeed and likely grow? Probably—but current valuation seems to be pricing in near dominance in a crowded field, including deep-pocket competitors. Falling out of favor could result in large losses, considering the 400% one-year gain in the share price.

Carvana—The Reports of (my) Death Are Greatly Exaggerated

With apologies to Mark Twain, Carvana did not go under as some people predicted over the past few years. Instead, they have reported good earnings and continue to grow as part of the new model of car sales, with more and more used car transactions happening away from dealerships or private individual sales.

The current optimism regarding Carvana is also exaggerated. Of all the companies featured in this article, Carvana does stand to benefit the most from the recent Fed rate cut and would really benefit if more cuts happen quickly, as consumer loan rates, especially car loans, are higher than most have seen in nearly 20 years. But this alone is not enough to warrant the optimism in Carvana since its lows around May of 2023. Not only has the share price gone up over 92% year-to-date, but it's also up 34x since "the sky is falling" pricing in the Spring of 2023. Their valuation metrics are D+ at best (per SA), and many are barely "passing" and Fs. With valuations across the market already high, Carvana's are exceptionally high. Just as the pessimism in 2023 was overdone, the optimism now is likely excessive. At a minimum, it might be time to be like a casino gambler on a winning streak—take the original stake off the table and let some winnings ride. As I'm a big fan of covered calls, a November 21, 2025 $400 call recently sold for a $35.93 premium. Yes, this indicates HUGE upside optimism (over 10% between capital gain and premium to be profitable), and while covered calls set an upside on gain, someone who has ridden Carvana would never go broke getting nearly $36 in call premium and $400 per share if called out. This is just an example and one way to reduce risk. And if not called out, additional covered calls at an acceptable capital gain could be sold.

This Time It's Different—Sort Of

This could be and might develop into a separate article.

Yes, I just typed the four most dangerous words in market analysis. But why do I, a conservative, fundamentals and value-oriented investor, finally admit there are some differences in the market? Definitely since Benjamin Graham wrote his tome, and even in my investing lifetime? Why are slightly higher than average valuations becoming the norm, despite this article being about stocks with outsize valuations?

As a quick summary, here are my reasons, although I do think the market is overbought right now:

1) The growth of passive investing/democratization of stock ownership starting in the 1980s.

2) Fact number 1 accelerated by the shift from pensions to 401(k)'s and other employee-based defined contribution retirement plans.

3) The growth of indexed funds and indexed exchange-traded funds, especially in valuation-weighted indexes, primarily the S&P 500. This causes a buy high, buy higher chasing of winners and ongoing narrowing of the dominant shares in the index, aka "The Magnificent Seven".

4) Lastly, "The Fed Put" and emotional/mental hangover from the Great Recession.

All of these work together to create and reinforce the "TINA" market we've seen, regardless of who is in office, de facto recessions, even if reporting makes it "just good enough" that one isn't reported, and events like COVID and a European and Palestinian war continuing with no near-term solution. I'm not Pollyanna enough to think we will never see a major bear market again, but I also have had to adjust my personal "band" of market valuation metrics.

That said, I do think there are certain companies, among them Hims & Hers, Palantir, and Carvana, that are priced way too optimistically presently. Could their prices stay high, especially if their next quarterly reports continue to show positive results? Yes, but I wouldn't initiate positions at these valuations and even look at ways to reduce risk, whether by selling a portion of a position to protect original capital or using covered calls.

Best wishes for investment success!

data.title: MapLight Therapeutics Starts $100 Million IPO Effort For CNS Treatments
data.description:
A Quick Take On MapLight Therapeutics

MapLight Therapeutics, Inc. (MPLT) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

The company is a clinical-stage biopharma developing treatments for various central nervous system diseases.

MPLT is currently in Phase 2 trials for its lead program but won’t have topline results until the second half of 2026 and 2027.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more IPO details.

MapLight Overview And Development Efforts

Redwood City, California-based MapLight Therapeutics was founded to develop a pipeline of treatments for debilitating CNS disorders, including schizophrenia, Alzheimer's Disease, and other nervous system conditions.

Management is led by co-founder and CEO Mr. Christopher A. Kroeger, M.D., who has been with the firm since its inception in 2018 and was previously CEO of OvaScience before its merger with Millendo Therapeutics and Cardioxyl Pharmaceuticals until its sale to Bristol-Myers Squibb.

The firm's lead candidate, ML-07C-MA, has proven to be well-tolerated with convenient dosing in Phase 1 trials for the treatment of schizophrenia and Alzheimer's Disease Psychosis [ADP].

The drug is currently in Phase 2 trials for each indication, with topline results for its schizophrenia trial expected in the second half of 2026 and for ADP in the second half of 2027.

The current status of the firm’s development pipeline is shown here:

MapLight has booked fair market value investment of $315.3 million as of June 30, 2025, from investors, including Catalyst4, NFLS Beta Limited, Novo Holdings A/S, and Forbion.

MapLight’s Markets & Competitors

According to a 2024 market research report by Grand View Research, the global market for schizophrenia drug treatments was an estimated $8.2 billion in 2024 and is forecast to exceed $11.1 billion by 2030.

This represents a forecast CAGR of 5.6% from 2025 to 2030.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are a continued moderate rise in the prevalence of schizophrenia disorders worldwide, along with additional treatment options for patients.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and projected growth path of the schizophrenia drugs market by region from 2018 to 2030:

The company is also developing treatments for other large markets with significant growth trajectories.

Major competitive companies that have developed or are developing similar treatments include the following firms:

  • Otsuka Pharmaceutical (OTCPK:OTSKY)

  • Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)

  • Bristol Myers-Squibb (BMY)

  • AbbVie (ABBV)

  • Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX)

  • Neumora Therapeutics (NMRA)

MapLight's Financial Results

The company’s recent financial results are typical of a development-stage biopharma, as they feature no revenue and substantial R&D and G&A expenses associated with its pipeline development efforts.

Here are the firm’s current financial results for the periods indicated, per its recent IPO filing documents:

As of June 30, 2025, the company had $60.5 million in cash and $20.4 million in total liabilities.

MapLight's IPO Details

MapLight intends to raise $100 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its common stock, although the final figure may differ.

No existing shareholders have shown an interest in acquiring shares of the IPO, at least in its initial S-1 filing.

Company, shareholder, and executive lock-ups will be for the usual 180-day period after the effective date of the prospectus.

Management said it plans to use the IPO net proceeds as detailed here:

To fund the clinical development of ML-007C-MA in schizophrenia through the completion of our ongoing Phase 2 ZEPHYR study;

To fund the clinical development of ML-007C-MA in ADP through the completion of our ongoing Phase 2 VISTA study;

To fund the clinical development of ML-004 in ASD through the completion of our ongoing Phase 2 IRIS study;

To fund other research and development activities, including preclinical studies for ML-009 and ML-021; and

The remainder for working capital and other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Leadership’s online company roadshow presentation is not yet available.

Pertaining to legal proceedings, management said the company is not subject to any litigation that is material to its operations.

Listed bookrunners of the IPO are Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, Leerink Partners, and Stifel.

My Thoughts About MapLight’s IPO

MPLT is seeking US public capital market investment to move its substantial CNS treatment pipeline through and into trials.

The firm’s lead candidate, ML-07C-MA, has proven to be well-tolerated with convenient dosing in Phase 1 trials for the treatment of schizophrenia and Alzheimer's Disease Psychosis [ADP].

So far, trial results have shown good tolerability and convenience, but we don’t have any initial feedback on efficacy, which will likely be better known in early 2026 for its lead Phase 2 trial for the treatment of schizophrenia.

The market opportunity for treating serious CNS disorders is large and expected to grow substantially in the coming years, although successful treatments are extremely difficult to predict, and there have been many failures in these difficult-to-understand areas of health treatment.

Management hasn’t disclosed any major pharma firm collaboration agreements or relationships but has indicated its interest in potential future collaboration arrangements.

The company’s investor syndicate includes two well-known life science venture capital firms, and the company CEO is highly experienced in the field.

MapLight is reasonably well capitalized but will require significantly higher levels of capital to meaningfully develop its rather large and varied pipeline of treatment options.

When we learn more about the IPO’s pricing and valuation expectations from management, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Cintas: Upped Guidance, Shares Back In The Buy Zone
data.description:
For many quarters, we have been bullish on shares of Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS). Our most recent turn bullish came following its plunge in December 2024. We had called for a buy at $185 in December. Most recently we moved to a neutral rating expecting shares to consolidate, and expected you might be able to get shares again under $200 which is clearly panning out. In our last column we said the chart suggested shares wanted to test $200 again, which is at this level and below and we thought new money could re-enter. But folks, little has changed in terms of how this trades.

On many basic metrics CTAS stock is "expensive," but the growth is quite positive despite the premium valuation. Shares remain expensive, but the premium here has existed for many quarters. The company remains a steady grower and has upped its guidance following the just reported fiscal Q1 earnings.

Cintas headline results

The headline results were not all that impressive relative to consensus estimates. The revenue line was a small beat of $20 million, while the EPS figure was perfectly in line with expectations. Given the premium valuation, this is admittedly underwhelming. Revenue was $2.72 billion, rising 8.8% compared to $2.50 billion a year ago. The organic revenue growth rate for fiscal Q1, which adjusts for the impacts of acquisitions and foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, was a strong 7.8%. It is also worth noting that revenue was up sequentially as well from fiscal Q4 by 2%.

Cintas margins

It is impossible for any company to grow margins forever and ever, mathematically. But for Cintas, the margin trend has been solid, and in fiscal Q1 we saw expansion once again. Gross margin hit $1.37 billion compared to $1.25 billion a year ago, an increase of 9.1%. This was up from $1.33 billion in the sequential quarter as well, which is extremely positive. Year-over-year comps are strong and seeing sequential growth is positive. A strong result here was that the gross margin as a percentage of revenue was 50.3%, rising 20 basis points from 50.1% a year ago. This was also a 60 basis point improvement sequentially. In our last coverage, we hypothesized that margins could get and stay over 50%. The first part of this 2-leg parlay has hit. Now we will see if margins can be maintained over 50%.

Cintas earnings power

Cintas is still in growth mode, but it is not hypergrowth. It is a reliable, steady growth. Operating income in fiscal Q1 was up 10.1% to $617.9 million vs. $561.0 million a year ago. Operating income was also up from the sequential quarter. The operating income margin as a percentage of sales was 22.7% in Q1 2026, compared to 22.4% a year ago. This was also a sequential increase from 22.4% in fiscal Q4. A solid result.

And with that, we saw earnings growth. Net income was $491.1 million, up 8.7% from last year. Overall, EPS hit $1.20, compared to $1.10 a year ago. So, growth remains but has slowed some at least in terms of the pace of growth. Many believe this growth does not justify the premium valuation, but the company does have a decent moat in our opinion and is shareholder-friendly.

Cintas is shareholder-friendly

Keep in mind that Cintas also pays a growing dividend and is buying back shares. The company just paid a total of $182 million in dividends this fiscal quarter, an increase of 15.4% from the amount paid last year for the same quarter. Sure, the yield is low, but we love long-term dividend growth. And the company bought back $347 million worth of stock in the quarter and in September. Note that it is half of the amount purchased in the prior fiscal year and has hit such a level quickly. It suggests the repurchases are being accelerated as shares have consolidated.

Looking ahead

As we look ahead to fiscal 2026, we continue to expect ongoing growth, and the big positive from the quarter is that the company revised guidance higher. Previously, Cintas saw revenue expanding to $11.0 to $11.15 billion, up from the $10.34 billion it brought in for fiscal 2025. It also saw $4.71 to $4.85 in EPS. Now it sees revenue hitting $11.06 to $11.18 and EPS tracking toward $4.74 to $4.86. This assumes no acquisitions in the fiscal year, no new repurchases, and no substantial swings in the economy.

This stock trades like a couple of others that are slow and steady growers but enjoy a premium valuation. Make no mistake even as we dip under $200 here, there remains a rich premium here. In short, you are paying up for this growth, the higher revisions, and the company's moat. Now that CTAS shares have dipped under $200, we think you can do some fresh buying in $10 blocks. This is an investing approach we teach at BAD BEAT Investing.
